properties.name: Echeandía opens partial emancipation
properties.summary: Gov. Echeandía's emancipation program begins: qualified neophytes of the southern missions may leave mission discipline, with individual segregation licenses following (Gil Riela and Andrés Ibarra among the first named). The 1826 San Diego dossier ties the friars' constitutional oath to neophyte emancipation and floats a neophyte pueblo at San Fernando or San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 27) Doc 127 (the 1826 oath-and-emancipation dossier).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 47) Doc 71 (segregation of Gil Riela, 30 Apr 1826).

properties.notes: The proclamation's own text is not in the vault surveys; its execution is. Standard accounts date the proclamation July 1826.

properties.name: Pre-secularization land census ordered
properties.summary: The Diputación orders every mission to report its lands and boundaries before year's end — the first territorial step toward disentailment. The resulting 1828 'Informes sobre terrenos y ganados' series, with each mission's cattle brand, survives in the State Papers: Missions volumes.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., Monterey (C-A 43) Doc 1 (bando, 7 Oct 1827).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Docs 402–412, 418 (the 1828 informes).

properties.name: Echeandía's secularization decree
properties.summary: 'Ley secularizando las Misiones, declarándolas pueblos' — 33 articles converting the missions to pueblos under the 1813 Cortes law, San Gabriel and San Carlos first, extended to all by art. 24. Read to the neophytes at the Monterey-district missions that January; suspended by Gov. Victoria within days, denounced to Mexico, and annulled by the central government in April 1832.

sources.citation: Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 224 (the 33 articles, complete).
St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 29 (the decree).
Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Docs 3–5 (promulgation to the neophytes, Jan 1831).

properties.notes: The C-A 49 copy sits after Nov-1831 letters in the volume; the surveys flag the year for verification against Bancroft/Hutchinson.

properties.name: The Mexican secularization law of Aug 17, 1833
properties.summary: The national statute: 'Sobre secularización de las misiones de Alta y Baja California,' converting each mission to a secular parish. The Californian archive's own copy survives in the Superior Government State Papers, with the 26 Nov 1833 colonization companion law that underwrote the Híjar-Padrés colony.

sources.citation: Sup. Govt. St. Pap. (C-A 57) Doc 93, orig. pp. 415–416.
Sup. Govt. St. Pap. (C-A 57) Doc 94 (the 26 Nov 1833 colonization law).

properties.name: Gómez Farías: all missions, four months
properties.summary: The general secularization decree from Mexico: ALL the republic's missions to be secularized within four months. It reached California as Figueroa and the Diputación were already drafting their own, more gradual machinery.

sources.citation: Sup. Govt. St. Pap. (C-A 57) Doc 95.

properties.name: Figueroa's Reglamento Provisional
properties.summary: The instrument that actually dismantled the system: missions partially converted to pueblos; each family head a solar of up to 400 varas and half the livestock; article 18 barring the Indians from alienating their grants; article 22 barring mass slaughter of herds. Enacted through the Diputación's article-by-article debate that July, with same-day instructions to the comisionados — general inventories, dissolution of the monjerío, rancherías of 25 families eligible to form pueblos.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 78, Sav. 253–257 (the printed reglamento).
C-A 53 Doc 79 (Instrucciones para los Comisionados, 12 prevenciones).
Leg. Rec. (C-A 60) Doc 2 (the Diputación debate, Jul 1834).
Leg. Rec. (C-A 59) Doc 31 (the c. 1828–30 'Plan para convertir en pueblos las Misiones', the Diputación blueprint).

properties.name: Alvarado's administrators' reglamento
properties.summary: A ~14-article code for the mission administrators — render accounts, no credit against mission property 'por ningún pretexto,' padrón of neophytes — the government's attempt to police the men it had put over the ex-mission estates. A further mission decree followed on 17 Apr 1839.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., San José (C-A 45) Doc 68 (the reglamento).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 639 (the 17 Apr 1839 decree, sent to Durán).

properties.notes: The standard accounts' 'Jan 17, 1839 regulation' matches neither vault instrument exactly; both are shown.

properties.name: Administrators suppressed, mayordomos created
properties.summary: Alvarado's 1 March 1840 decree suppresses the mission administrators and replaces them with mayordomos under the Visitador's oversight — the government's verdict on six years of administrator rule. Emancipation power was reserved exclusively to the government that October.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., LA: Decrees & Despatches (C-A 36) Doc 63, tomo p. 155.
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 646 (16 copies to Durán, 8 Apr 1840).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 596 (emancipation reserved to the government, 23 Oct 1840).

properties.name: Micheltorena returns 12 missions to the friars
properties.summary: The reversal: twelve named missions returned to the padres 'como en tutoría de los Indígenas,' remitting an eighth of their products — after a February order suspending all further alienation of mission lands. The 3 April circular ordering administrators to hand over names ten of them.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 175, Sav. 669–674.
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 1043 (the handover circular, 3 Apr 1843).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 1016 (the Feb 1843 alienation freeze).

properties.name: Pico's sale-and-lease reglamento
properties.summary: The 23-article 'Reglamento para la enajenación y arriendo de las Misiones': sell San Rafael, Dolores, Soledad, San Miguel, and La Purísima; lease the rest for nine years. Chapter 4 declares the neophytes freed and 'no estando obligados á servir á los arrendatarios'; article 20 lets Indians petition for inalienable title to their house and garden plots. Andrés Pico and Juan Manso had been inventorying the missions since May — the commission's object expressly their arrendamiento.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., LA: Decrees & Despatches (C-A 36) Doc 107 (verbatim, 23 arts.).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 1225 (the commission's lease mandate, 1 Jul 1845).
C-A 53 Doc 191 (Pico & Manso commissioned, 11 Jul 1845).

properties.name: Mexico orders the sales suspended
properties.summary: The supreme government, learning what the Department 'ha dispuesto' with the missions, orders all alienation proceedings reported and suspended. The sales went ahead anyway.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 181.

properties.name: The Assembly authorizes outright sale
properties.summary: Pico's government, invoking the common defense 'en caso de una invasión extranjera,' authorizes auctioning the missions to pay their creditors, any surplus to each mission's Indian community. The June 1846 deeds of sale cite this decree as their authority.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., LA: Decrees & Despatches (C-A 36) Doc 110.
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 160 (the San Buenaventura escritura quoting the 13 Apr decree).

properties.name: The Assembly annuls Pico's sales
properties.summary: With the American conquest underway, the Departmental Assembly annuls the mission sales (art. 1) while continuing the leases (art. 3). The U.S. courts finished the job: the Supreme Court later voided the San Gabriel and San Luis Rey sales.

sources.citation: Unbound Docs. (C-A 63) Doc 479 (verbatim abstract).
U.S. v. Workman (1863) per Caragozian, CSCHS (standard account).

properties.name: Mission San Diego de Alcalá
properties.summary: Secularized under the 1834 program; José Joaquín Ortega administered the ex-mission from April 1835. Sold to Santiago Argüello in the last weeks of Mexican rule.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Docs 57–58 (inventory & plano).
Unbound Docs. (C-A 63) Doc 393 (the Argüello sale, 8 Jun 1846).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: An 1847 charge alleged the Argüello bill of sale was ante-dated (C-A 63 Doc 190).
series: Salary roll: Ortega $600/yr; mayordomo Rosario Aguilar $18/month
Inventory with a hand-drawn plano of the ex-mission; district padrón 780–796 neophytes

properties.name: Mission San Luis Rey
properties.summary: The richest inventory in the record: $203,787 existencia across the mission and its ranchos (Pala, Santa Margarita, Temécula, San Jacinto). Pío Pico, first a comisionado, administered it to 1840 — removed only under threat of punishment — and its Luiseño residents mounted the most sustained documented resistance to the new order.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 15 (the 1835 general inventory).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 657 (the forced handover order, 23 Jul 1840).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The Cot & Pico sale of 1846 is not in the vault surveys; the July 1846 Marrón handover is.
series: Comisionados Pablo de la Portilla & Pío Pico
Pico compelled to hand over the mission 'else be punished for disobedience'
Returned to Fr. Zalvidea via José Antonio Estudillo
Handed to Juan M. Marrón on Pico's order

properties.name: Mission San Juan Capistrano
properties.summary: The earliest secularization — Echeandía's pilot — and in 1841 the first ex-mission formally converted to a pueblo, with a 17-article reglamento and a land repartimiento naming its neófitos libres. Sold in December 1845 for $710.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161 (Pico's consolidated alienation record).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The two vault sale records disagree on the second buyer — 'James McKinley' (C-A 51 Doc 161) vs 'Santiago Machado' (the título, C-A 63 Doc 508c). Both shown; unresolved.
series: Administrators Francisco Sepúlveda, then Santiago Argüello ($1,000/yr)
Pueblo conversion under comisionado Juan Bandini; 17-article reglamento; repartimiento of 7,950 varas
Sold to John Forster & partner, $710 'en moneda, cueros y sebo'

properties.name: Mission San Gabriel Arcángel
properties.summary: Handed from Fr. Estenaga to comisionado Nicolás Gutiérrez in November 1834 with 12,980 cattle on its books; by the 1840 inventory the herd had collapsed to 72. Leased under Pico's program — the lease drive began here — and in the vault record assigned to Henry Dalton in February 1846.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 16 (the 1834 handover accounts).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 54 (the 1840 inventory).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The Reid & Workman sale of June 1846 is not in the vault surveys as read; the U.S. Supreme Court later voided the San Gabriel sale (standard account).
series: Entrega Fr. Estenaga → Nicolás Gutiérrez; debts $9,474.36
Partial inventory: 12,980 cattle, 6,548 sheep, 2,938 horses; 1,323 souls
Inventory: 72 cattle, 715 sheep — the estate stripped
Returned to Fr. Estenaga under Micheltorena's decree

properties.name: Mission San Fernando Rey
properties.summary: Entrega to comisionado Antonio del Valle in December 1834; a decade later, leased to Andrés Pico and Juan Manso, then sold to Eulogio Célis in June 1846 for $14,000 'para sostener la integridad del Territorio.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 445 (the 1834 entrega).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: Lease figures differ between the two vault records ($1,120 vs $1,125); both shown.
series: Entrega to comisionado Antonio del Valle
Leased to Andrés Pico & Juan Manso ($1,120/yr; one record $1,125 split gov't/padre/neophytes)

properties.name: Mission San Buenaventura
properties.summary: Among the last secularized: Gov. Chico ordered Fr. Blas Ordaz to hand it to Carlos Carrillo in June 1836 amid the constitutional-oath fight. Sold to its lessee José Arnaz in June 1846 — 'dueño legítimo… para siempre' — followed by an 1846–48 asset-stripping dossier.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 157 (the Chico expediente).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 160 (the deed of sale).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The 1846–48 fraud dossier on the lease is C-A 63 Doc 464.
series: Chico orders handover to Carlos Carrillo
Leased to Narciso Botello & José Arnaz, $1,630/yr
Sold to José Arnaz (escritura quoting the 13 Apr defense decree)

properties.name: Mission Santa Bárbara
properties.summary: Comisionado Anastacio Carrillo took the richest coastal plant in the 1835 inventory — $113,960 existencia including Rancho Dos Pueblos — with $2,484 in goods distributed to the Indians. Leased in December 1845 to Nicolás Den and Daniel Hill.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 443 (the 1835 inventory).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Leased to Nicolás A. Den & Daniel Hill, $1,260/yr

properties.name: Mission Santa Inés
properties.summary: Secularized in the 1836 wave on Chico's order; Fr. Jimeno handed it to José María Covarrubias that August with an existencia of $56,437 — down to $39,284 within six months. Leased in 1845 to Covarrubias himself with Joaquín Carrillo.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 36 (the 1836 inventory).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Chico orders handover to José Mª Ramírez
Fr. Jimeno → José María Covarrubias; inventory $56,437, 8,040 cattle
Leased to José M. Covarrubias & Joaquín Carrillo, $580/yr

properties.name: Mission La Purísima Concepción
properties.summary: Inventoried under article 15 of Figueroa's reglamento by comisionado Domingo Carrillo, with William Dana as appraiser: existencia $62,088.50 including ranchos Todos Santos and Guadalupe. One of the five missions Pico's reglamento listed for outright sale — bought by John Temple for $10,110 in December 1845.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 439 (the 1834 inventory).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The survey flags the inventory digits as scale-2 reads pending verification.
series: Comisionado Domingo Carrillo; avaluador William Dana; existencia $62,088.50

properties.name: Mission San Luis Obispo
properties.summary: A revolving door of administrators (Trujillo, Moreno, Ayala, Canet, Bonilla); 253 souls in 1834. Erected into a pueblo in July 1844 — its emancipados barred from selling their lands — then sold in December 1845 to Scott, Wilson & McKinley.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The $510 price is the standard account; the vault record gives the buyers, not the price.
series: Land reparto to the Indians ordered
Erected into a pueblo; emancipados may not sell their lands
Sold to Scott, Wilson & James McKinley

properties.name: Mission San Miguel Arcángel
properties.summary: In January 1831 its neophytes refused Echeandía's secularization outright — 'quieren seguir bajo el actual sistema' — and in 1831 asked to remain 'en estado de Comunidad.' Listed for outright sale in Pico's reglamento; the vault holds no sale record.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Doc 3 (the neophytes' refusal, Jan 1831).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 128 (the 1837 inventory).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The 1846 Ríos & Reed purchase is the standard account; not found in the vault surveys.
series: Neophytes refuse secularization: 'quieren seguir bajo el actual sistema'
Listed to SELL in Pico's reglamento

properties.name: Mission San Antonio de Padua
properties.summary: Fr. Pedro Cabot delivered the mission to comisionado Manuel Crespo in November 1834; the 1836 inventory totaled $93,122 across eleven named ranchos, with $13,602 distributed to the Indians and a 'Pueblo de San Antonio' of 557 souls. Offered in 1845, it drew no bidders and was never sold.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 448 (the 1834 entrega).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Entrega Fr. Cabot → comisionado Manuel Crespo
$13,602.37 distributed to the Indians; 'Pueblo de San Antonio — 557 souls'

properties.name: Mission Nuestra Señora de la Soledad
properties.summary: Inventoried in August 1835 (~$47,297 — Savage himself noted the figures don't reconcile); 177 souls by 1837. Sold in June 1846 to Feliciano Soberanes, the same encargado instructed in 1840 that 'ningún indígena será forzado á servir á un particular.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 161.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Encargado Feliciano Soberanes: no Indian to be forced to serve a private party

properties.name: Mission San Carlos Borromeo (Carmel)
properties.summary: One of the two missions Echeandía's 1831 decree named to go first. By 1839 juez interino Marcelino Escobar held the buildings under a room-by-room inventory, with solares assigned — two to the neófitos Josecillo and Antonio. Its surplus buildings were saleable under the 1845 reglamento; no sale appears in the vault.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., Juzgados (C-A 33) Doc 8 (the 1839 inventory).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Building inventory under juez interino Marcelino Escobar; solares to neófitos
Surplus buildings saleable under Pico's reglamento

properties.name: Mission San Juan Bautista
properties.summary: Handed over in May 1835 with a $147,413 existencia including Rancho San Felipe, and a padrón of 63 emancipated Indians holding $5,120 in land. An 1849 retrospective recorded it 'declarada pueblo' a dozen years earlier, its mission houses destroyed for materials.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 14 (the 1835 handover).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Handover; comisionados Antonio Buelna & José T. Castro; church appraisal
Retrospective: 'declarada pueblo' 12+ years earlier; houses destroyed for materials

properties.name: Mission Santa Cruz
properties.summary: Comisionado Ignacio del Valle inventoried ~$84,354 in December 1835. Listed for outright sale in Pico's reglamento; no sale appears in the vault — and in 1849 Hartnell judged that the priest 'has no right at all to sell any real estate or the mission Sta Cruz.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 452 (the 1835 inventory).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Inventory + avalúo: existencia ~$84,354.62 (comisionado Ignacio del Valle)
Listed to SELL in Pico's reglamento
Hartnell: the priest has no right to sell the mission's real estate

properties.name: Mission Santa Clara de Asís
properties.summary: Administered by the Estradas, then Ignacio Alviso, whose 1840 handover inventory counted 3,847 cattle (down from 5,500 the year before). Its mission houses were barred from sale in 1845; in 1849 the priests leased Santa Clara and San José together to James D. Steinberger for $22,000.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 99 (the 1840 handover).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Handover Estrada → Ignacio Alviso: 3,847 cattle, 4,807 sheep
Sta Clara + S. José leased to James D. Steinberger, $22,000

properties.name: Mission San José
properties.summary: Still the great stock estate of the north after secularization — José de Jesús Vallejo's 1840 receipt counted 20,000 cattle and 15,000 sheep — while draining $16,809 to the government. Hartnell's 1840 visita found 589 souls who 'quieren otro administrador.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 143 (Hartnell's San José visita).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The reported 1845/46 sale is disputed between the standard accounts and not in the vault; left open.
series: Ex-admin J. J. Vallejo → José Mª de Jesús González
Receipt from Vallejo: 20,000 cattle, 15,000 sheep, 520 horses
Leased with Santa Clara to Steinberger

properties.name: Mission San Francisco de Asís (Dolores)
properties.summary: Inventoried at $67,227 in July 1835 under comisionados Ignacio del Valle and José Joaquín Estudillo; by the 1841 padrón, 78 souls remained. One of the five listed for outright sale in 1845; no sale record survives in the vault.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 27 (the 1835 inventory).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

series: Ex-admin José de la Cruz Sánchez → mayordomo Tiburcio Vásquez, via Hartnell
Listed to SELL in Pico's reglamento

properties.name: Mission San Rafael Arcángel
properties.summary: The poorest inventory of the set ($18,474, with Rancho Nicasio at $7,256), but the most direct distribution: 343 Indians received rams, ewes, horses, and mares by name. By Hartnell's 1840 visita they refused a mayordomo altogether — 'no querían ya misión.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 50) Doc 458 (the 1834 inventory).
St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 149 (Hartnell's San Rafael report).
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: The reported $8,000 sale of 1846 is the standard account; no vault record.
series: 343 Indians receive 463 rams, 828 ewes, 97 horses, 343 mares
Indians refuse mayordomo Briones: 'no querían ya misión'

properties.name: Mission San Francisco Solano (Sonoma)
properties.summary: Secularized under M. G. Vallejo as comisionado (standard account); the vault's mission-property record names Vallejo, Salvador Vallejo, and mayordomo Antonio Ortega, and shows the mission's stock drawn down for the frontier colony and recruitment in 1835. Its final disposition does not appear in the vault surveys.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Doc 140.
Secularization year per the standard accounts (Krell; cross-checked against CA State Parks / NPS) — year grain.

properties.notes: Excluded (with San Rafael) from the Diputación's c. 1828–30 pueblo plan as too new.
series: M. G. Vallejo comisionado; Salvador Vallejo & mayordomo Antonio Ortega in the property record
Vallejo 'ocupado en recluta, ración de la Misión y matanzas'

properties.name: The Híjar-Padrés colony lands at San Diego
properties.summary: The brigantine Natalia — the Compañía Cosmopolitana's own ship — reaches San Diego with Director General José María Híjar, Buenaventura Araujo, and 129 colonists, the vanguard of the ~204-person colonization scheme budgeted at 45,206 pesos under the 26 Nov 1833 law. The mission estates were to be its endowment.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Doc 243 (the arrival report, 4 Sep 1834).
St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Docs 51–53, 90–93 (appointment, roster, budget).

properties.name: Figueroa keeps the missions: the contramando
properties.summary: Santa Anna's countermand — carried north 'por extraordinario violento' — orders Figueroa not to hand Híjar the political command; the Diputación's acuerdo goes further: neither the command 'ni los bienes de las Misiones, que son propiedad de los indios.' The colony is stranded, and the principle that the estates belong to the Indians is stated at its plainest.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., LA: Official Corr. (C-A 37) Doc 73 (the countermand, leaf-verified).
C-A 37 Doc 78 (the Diputación acuerdo, 22 Oct 1834).

properties.name: Araujo and the armed rising at San Gabriel
properties.summary: In the colony crisis's ugliest episode, ~200 armed Indians briefly seize Fr. Estenaga at San Gabriel; colony officer Buenaventura Araujo is charged with instigating the rising. Ten northern missions are nonetheless ordered to provision the stranded colonists for a year.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Docs 249, 253, 257–260 (the Araujo proceedings).

properties.name: Híjar and Padrés deported on the Loriot
properties.summary: After the Los Angeles plot to depose Figueroa and halt secularization, the colony's leadership is shipped to Mexico: the Loriot's six-article instructions name Híjar, Padrés, Francisco Torres, and Apalátegui among the deportees. Figueroa's manifesto had already given the government's verdict: 'el verdadero y único objeto de su plan es el robo de los bienes de las misiones.' Padrés protested his rights from on board.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 29) Doc 25 (the Loriot instructions).
Dep. St. Pap., Benicia (C-A 39) Docs 187–…, and C-A 29 Docs 11–30 (the crisis dossier).

properties.name: Hartnell named Visitador General
properties.summary: William Hartnell is named Visitador of the missions at $2,000 a year — the government's inspector over its own administrators — his salary apportioned mission by mission. His two circuits of 1839–40 produced the most unsparing record of the ex-mission estates that survives.

sources.citation: Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 397 (the appointment).
Dep. Rec. (C-A 49) Doc 434 (the salary apportionment).

properties.notes: He resigned 6 Oct 1840 and ceased functions 7 Nov 1840 (C-A 49 Doc 660; C-A 51 Doc 150).
series: Takes the oath; departs on the southern circuit in April
His Instructions to Administrators: punishment capped at 25 lashes; no reprisals against Indians who complain

properties.name: The southern visita: 'los indios son acreedores'
properties.summary: Hartnell's report on the missions from San Diego to San Fernando: the San Dieguito Indians 'son acreedores á la tierra que reclaman'; San Juan Capistrano's community memorial against administrator Argüello arrives 'firmado por José Fermín á nombre de la comunidad'; San Fernando counts 416 souls, its Rancho San Francisco 'taken from them and given to Antonio del Valle.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 151 (the 24 Jun 1839 report).

properties.name: San Rafael: 'no querían ya misión'
properties.summary: On the northern circuit Hartnell finds the San Rafael Indians refusing mayordomo Gregorio Briones and refusing the institution itself — 'no querían ya misión.' His 14 May report to Jimeno Casarín is the keystone document of Indian liberty in the northern ex-missions.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 149 (the San Rafael report).

properties.name: Vallejo arrests the Visitador
properties.summary: At Yerba Buena, M. G. Vallejo intercepts and arrests Hartnell rather than let the inspection reach his Sonoma frontier arrangements — the plainest demonstration of where power over the ex-mission estates actually lay.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 149 (reported with the San Rafael visitation).

properties.name: San José visita: 589 souls, 50 lashes
properties.summary: Hartnell counts 589 souls at Mission San José, records punishments running to 50 lashes — double his own 25-lash cap — and the Indians' verdict: 'quieren otro administrador.'

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 143.

properties.name: San Diego district: Pico, Temecula, and inventory fraud
properties.summary: The southern return visit: Pico has told the Indians the governor gave him Temecula; Las Flores brings its complaints; and comisionado Carlos Castro's inventory fraud is recorded — the Visitador's file on how the estates were actually being run.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 148 (the 26 Aug 1840 report).

properties.name: San Miguel refuses secularization
properties.summary: Read Echeandía's bando, the San Miguel neophytes answer that 'quieren seguir bajo el actual sistema' — while San Luis Obispo's elect four Indian comisarios de policía under it. The record of divided Indian judgments on emancipation, at the very start.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Docs 3–5 (the promulgation record).

properties.notes: Later (1831) they ask to remain 'en estado de Comunidad.'

properties.name: The southern missions demand the promised land
properties.summary: Neophytes of San Diego, San Juan Capistrano, San Gabriel, and San Luis Rey mobilize to demand the land distribution Echeandía had promised — proclaiming the reformer governor against his replacement. Secularization's first mass Indian politics.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., LA (C-A 34) Doc 330.
Dep. St. Pap. (C-A 28) Docs 115–116.

properties.name: 'Somos libres': San Luis Rey refuses to work
properties.summary: Portilla's reports from San Luis Rey: the Luiseños refuse absolutely to return to mission labor — 'somos libres, no queremos volver, no queremos trabajar.' The plainest statement of what emancipation meant to the emancipated.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 156 (Portilla → Figueroa).

properties.name: 'Que no nos pelen las nalgas á azotes'
properties.summary: The San Juan Capistrano neophytes — fewer than 60 still working, the rest fleeing — petition Alvarado against their administrator in their own unsparing words: that they not be flogged raw. Received weeks into Hartnell's visitaduría, it is the Indian-voice document of the administrator era.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 47 (the petition).

properties.name: The Las Flores bargain
properties.summary: Figueroa's oficio records the pueblo de Las Flores — the neophyte pueblo created from San Luis Rey's asistencia — accepting liberty in exchange for subsisting on its own labor. One of the few documented free-Indian pueblos of the secularization.

sources.citation: Unbound Docs. (C-A 63) Doc 15 (transcribing the 29 May 1835 oficio).

properties.name: Temecula and Pala stand armed against the Picos
properties.summary: When the Picos moved on Temecula — Pico telling the Indians the governor had given it to him — the juzgado record shows the rancherías in arms: 'toda la gente está armada.' The Luiseño ranchos defended the land through the juzgado and, when needed, with weapons in hand.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions (C-A 51) Doc 141 (the juzgado record).
Dep. St. Pap., Benicia Mil. (C-A 20) Doc 226 (the Temecula land conspiracy record: the land 'propiedad de todos').

properties.name: José Cupertino petitions for absolute emancipation
properties.summary: A sixty-year-old Santa Clara man, in service since childhood, petitions for his absolute emancipation — the individual face of the paper freedom the decade had constructed.

sources.citation: Dep. St. Pap., Prefecturas y Juzgados (C-A 42) Doc 687.

properties.name: 'Como á hija de la Misión': Feliciana Valdés claims land
properties.summary: An Indian woman petitions for her share of the secularized lands 'como á hija de la Misión' — a daughter's claim on the estate. Hartnell denied it; the file preserves a woman's legal argument for what secularization had promised.

sources.citation: St. Pap., Missions & Colonization (C-A 53) Docs 168–172.
